description: Boundary setting techniques and examples for counseling

boundary_types.definition: Limits around personal space, touch, and bodily autonomy
boundary_types.description: Physical boundaries encompass your right to personal space, physical touch, and bodily autonomy. These boundaries protect your physical wellbeing and communicate how you expect to be treated physically. Research shows that people with clear physical boundaries report higher self-esteem and lower anxiety in social situations (Brown, 2021).
boundary_types.psychological_background: Physical boundaries are rooted in attachment theory and self-determination theory. Early experiences with caregivers shape our comfort with physical proximity and touch. Trauma survivors often need additional support establishing physical boundaries (van der Kolk, 2014). The APA recognizes physical boundaries as fundamental to therapeutic relationships and client autonomy.

examples.user_says: My aunt always insists on hugging me even though I've told her I'm not comfortable with it. She says I'm being cold.
examples.boundary_response: You have every right to decide who touches your body and how. You might say: 'Aunt Maria, I appreciate your affection, but I'm not comfortable with hugs. I'd prefer a wave or handshake.'
examples.i_statement: I feel uncomfortable when you insist on hugging me because physical touch isn't something I'm comfortable with. I would like us to find another way to greet each other.
examples.dear_man_version: Aunt Maria, I care about our relationship and I want to explain something important. When we greet each other, I feel anxious about physical contact. I've been thinking about this and I need you to respect my preference for not hugging. I understand you show love through touch, and I appreciate that. Would you be open to a handshake or wave instead? This would really help me feel more comfortable at family gatherings.
examples.why_it_works: This approach validates the aunt's intentions while clearly stating your needs. It offers an alternative that maintains connection without compromising your boundaries.

examples.user_says: My colleague keeps touching my shoulder or back when talking to me. It makes me uncomfortable but I don't want to cause problems at work.
examples.boundary_response: Workplace physical boundaries are legally protected and essential for professional comfort. You might say: 'I appreciate the friendly gestures, but I prefer to keep physical contact minimal at work. I focus better when we maintain professional space.'
examples.i_statement: I feel distracted and uncomfortable when there's physical contact during conversations because it affects my ability to focus. I would like us to keep our interactions professional.
examples.dear_man_version: I wanted to talk about something that's been bothering me. I've noticed that physical contact like touching my shoulder happens during our conversations. I need us to keep interactions professional without physical contact. I value our working relationship, and maintaining professional boundaries helps me do my best work. Would you be willing to respect this going forward?
examples.why_it_works: This frames the boundary as professional necessity rather than personal rejection, reducing defensiveness while maintaining clarity.

examples.user_says: My partner goes through my phone and bags without asking. I feel like I have no privacy.
examples.boundary_response: Personal space includes your belongings. You might say: 'I need you to ask before going through my things. My phone and personal items are private, and I need that space respected.'
examples.i_statement: I feel violated and anxious when my personal belongings are searched without permission because it feels like a breach of trust. I would like you to ask before accessing my personal items.
examples.dear_man_version: I want to talk about something important for our relationship. When you go through my phone or bags without asking, I feel like my privacy isn't respected. I need us to agree that personal belongings are off-limits without permission. I understand curiosity, and I'm happy to share things when asked directly. Can we agree on this boundary?
examples.why_it_works: This establishes that privacy is healthy in relationships while offering transparency as an alternative to surveillance.

examples.user_says: My 14-year-old doesn't want me to hug them in front of friends anymore. I feel hurt.
examples.boundary_response: This is a healthy developmental boundary. You might say: 'I understand you're growing up and want more independence with your friends. I respect that. We can have our hugs at home.'
examples.i_statement: I feel a little sad because hugging has been our way of showing affection, but I understand you need different things as you grow. I would like us to find other ways to connect that feel comfortable for you.
examples.dear_man_version: I want you to know I respect your growing independence. When you don't want hugs in front of friends, I understand you're asserting your autonomy. I need us to maintain our connection even as boundaries change. Can we talk about other ways to show affection that feel comfortable for you?
examples.why_it_works: This validates the adolescent's developmental need for autonomy while maintaining the relationship connection.

common_mistakes: Apologizing excessively for having physical boundaries
Allowing guilt to override your comfort
Assuming others should know your boundaries without being told
Thinking boundaries mean rejection rather than self-care
things_to_avoid: Physical contact you're not comfortable with to 'be nice'
Letting others convince you your boundaries are wrong
Using aggression or hostility when setting physical boundaries
Ignoring your body's signals of discomfort
references: Brown, B. (2021). Atlas of the Heart
Linehan, M. M. (2015). DBT Skills Training Manual
van der Kolk, B. (2014). The Body Keeps the Score

boundary_types.definition: Limits on emotional labor, responsibility for others' feelings, and vulnerability sharing
boundary_types.description: Emotional boundaries involve recognizing that you are responsible for your own emotions and others are responsible for theirs. This includes how much emotional labor you take on, how much you share about yourself, and how you respond to others' emotional states. Research indicates that clear emotional boundaries correlate with reduced burnout and improved relationship satisfaction (Brown, 2021).
boundary_types.psychological_background: Emotional boundaries develop through secure attachment and are essential for emotional differentiation—the ability to maintain your sense of self while in close relationships. Enmeshment, where emotional boundaries are unclear, is associated with anxiety and codependency. DBT emphasizes emotional regulation as foundational to effective interpersonal interactions (Linehan, 2015).

examples.user_says: My friend calls me every day to complain about her problems but never asks how I'm doing. I'm exhausted.
examples.boundary_response: You can care about someone without being their sole emotional support. You might say: 'I care about you, but I'm not able to be your only support right now. Have you considered talking to a therapist or counselor?'
examples.i_statement: I feel drained and overwhelmed when I'm the only person you talk to about your problems because I don't have the emotional capacity for this alone. I would like us to find other support options for you.
examples.dear_man_version: I want to be honest because our friendship matters to me. When I'm the only person you share your struggles with daily, I feel overwhelmed and unable to support you well. I need us to find additional support—maybe a therapist or support group. I care about you, and having more support would help you more than I can alone. Can we explore those options together?
examples.why_it_works: This frames the boundary as caring for both parties rather than rejecting the friend.

examples.user_says: My mother says things like 'After all I've done for you' whenever I set a boundary. I feel so guilty.
examples.boundary_response: Guilt-tripping is a form of emotional manipulation, even when unintentional. You might say: 'Mom, I love you and appreciate everything you've done. I also need to make choices that are right for me. Both can be true.'
examples.i_statement: I feel guilty and conflicted when my decisions are met with guilt because it makes me question myself. I would like us to discuss things openly without guilt.
examples.dear_man_version: Mom, I need to talk about something important. When I set boundaries and you respond with 'after all I've done for you,' I feel guilty and conflicted. I appreciate everything you've done, and I also need to make my own choices. I need us to discuss things respectfully even when we disagree. Can we find a way to communicate without guilt?
examples.why_it_works: This acknowledges the relationship while refusing to accept guilt as a manipulation tactic.

examples.user_says: My partner comes home and immediately starts venting about work. I can't even process my own day.
examples.boundary_response: Partners can support each other while also protecting their own emotional space. You might say: 'I want to hear about your day, but I need 20 minutes to decompress first. Then I'm all yours.'
examples.i_statement: I feel overwhelmed when I'm hit with work stress immediately because I need time to transition. I would like us to have a brief decompression time before sharing our days.
examples.dear_man_version: I love that you trust me with your work frustrations. When I'm immediately hit with stress as soon as you get home, I feel overwhelmed because I haven't decompressed yet. I need about 20 minutes to transition from work mode. Then I'm fully present to listen. Can we try this routine?
examples.why_it_works: This suggests a practical solution while validating the partner's need to share.

examples.user_says: My family expects me to drop everything when they need help. I feel like I have no life of my own.
examples.boundary_response: Being available doesn't mean being available always. You might say: 'I'm happy to help when I can, but I need to check my schedule first. I can't always drop everything immediately.'
examples.i_statement: I feel resentful and burnt out when I'm expected to always be available because I lose my own time. I would like to be able to choose when I can help.
examples.dear_man_version: I want to help family when I can, but I need to set realistic expectations. When I'm expected to drop everything immediately, I feel overwhelmed and resentful. I need to be able to check my schedule and commit to times that work. I'm happy to help—just give me the courtesy of asking in advance when possible. Can we agree on this?
examples.why_it_works: This maintains helpfulness while establishing that your time has value.

examples.user_says: My sister blames me for her bad moods. She says I'm the reason she's upset.
examples.boundary_response: You are not responsible for others' emotional states. You might say: 'I can't control how you feel. I'm responsible for my actions, not your emotions. Let's talk about what's really going on.'
examples.i_statement: I feel helpless and attacked when my sister blames me for her emotions because I can't control how she feels. I would like us to each take responsibility for our own feelings.
examples.dear_man_version: I need to address something that's been hurting our relationship. When you blame me for your bad moods, I feel helpless because I'm not responsible for your emotions. I care about you and want to help, but I need us to each own our feelings. Can we talk about what's really bothering you without blame?
examples.why_it_works: This establishes emotional differentiation while keeping the door open for genuine support.

common_mistakes: Taking responsibility for others' emotions
Over-explaining your emotional boundaries
Feeling guilty for protecting your emotional energy
Confusing empathy with emotional absorption
things_to_avoid: Suppressing your emotions to keep the peace
Letting others define how you should feel
Taking on emotional labor as proof of love
Ignoring emotional exhaustion until burnout
references: Brown, B. (2021). Atlas of the Heart
Linehan, M. M. (2015). DBT Skills Training Manual
Alberti, R., & Emmons, M. (2017). Your Perfect Right

boundary_types.definition: Limits on how you spend your time and energy
boundary_types.description: Time boundaries involve protecting your schedule, commitments, and downtime. Clear time boundaries prevent overcommitment and burnout. Research shows that people who protect their time report higher life satisfaction and lower stress (Linehan, 2015). Time boundaries are particularly challenging in cultures that equate busyness with importance.
boundary_types.psychological_background: Time boundaries relate to self-care and energy management. Poor time boundaries often stem from people-pleasing patterns, fear of rejection, or unclear priorities. DBT's PLEASE skills emphasize balancing responsibilities with self-care. Cognitive behavioral approaches address the distorted thoughts that drive overcommitment (Alberti & Emmons, 2017).

examples.user_says: My boss expects me to answer emails at 10 PM. I feel like I never get a break.
examples.boundary_response: Work-life boundaries are essential for mental health. You might say: 'I check emails during work hours and respond promptly then. For urgent matters, a phone call is best.'
examples.i_statement: I feel burned out and unable to recharge when work communications extend into my personal time because I never get true downtime. I would like to establish clear work hours.
examples.dear_man_version: I want to discuss something important for my performance and wellbeing. When I receive work emails at 10 PM, I feel obligated to respond immediately, which leads to burnout. I do excellent work during business hours. For true emergencies, a phone call works better. Otherwise, I'll respond first thing the next morning. Can we agree on this approach?
examples.why_it_works: This reframes boundaries as performance-enhancing rather than restrictive.

examples.user_says: My friend is always 30-60 minutes late. I feel disrespected and like my time doesn't matter.
examples.boundary_response: Time boundaries show self-respect. You might say: 'I'll wait 15 minutes, but if you're not here by then, I'll go ahead without you. My time is valuable too.'
examples.i_statement: I feel disrespected and frustrated when I'm left waiting because it communicates that my time isn't important. I would like us to commit to punctuality.
examples.dear_man_version: I value our friendship and need to address something. When you're consistently late, I feel disrespected and like my time doesn't matter. I need to know our plans will start on time. I'll wait 15 minutes going forward, but then I'll continue with my day. This isn't about being rigid—it's about mutual respect. Can you commit to being on time?
examples.why_it_works: This establishes a clear, enforceable consequence while maintaining the relationship.

examples.user_says: I've said yes to every volunteer request at my kid's school. I'm exhausted and resentful.
examples.boundary_response: You can be helpful without being indispensable. You might say: 'I've been volunteering a lot and need to scale back. I can commit to one event per month.'
examples.i_statement: I feel exhausted and resentful when I overcommit because I lose time for myself and my family. I would like to limit my volunteer commitments.
examples.dear_man_version: I want to continue supporting the school, but I've overcommitted and need to adjust. When I say yes to everything, I burn out and can't give my best. I can commit to one event per month. I care about the school community, and sustainable involvement serves everyone better. Can we find a balance that works?
examples.why_it_works: This reframes boundaries as sustainability rather than selfishness.

examples.context: Partner demanding all your free time
examples.user_says: My partner wants to spend every weekend together. I need some alone time but feel guilty.
examples.boundary_response: Healthy relationships include individual space. You might say: 'I love our time together, and I also need some time alone to recharge. Let's plan our weekends to include both.'
examples.i_statement: I feel suffocated and guilty when all my time is shared because I need solitude to recharge. I would like us to balance together time with individual time.
examples.dear_man_version: I want to talk about how we spend our weekends. I love our time together, and I also need some time alone to recharge. When all weekends are shared, I feel overwhelmed. I need us to plan weekends that include both couple time and individual time. This will actually make me more present when we're together. Can we find this balance?
examples.why_it_works: This frames alone time as relationship-enhancing rather than avoiding.

examples.user_says: My manager gives me impossible deadlines. I'm always stressed and working overtime.
examples.boundary_response: Professional boundaries include realistic workload expectations. You might say: 'I want to deliver quality work. Let's discuss priorities so I can focus on what's most important within realistic timeframes.'
examples.i_statement: I feel stressed and overwhelmed when deadlines are unrealistic because it affects my work quality. I would like us to discuss priorities and timelines together.
examples.dear_man_version: I want to do excellent work, and I need to discuss something about our workflow. When I receive tight deadlines on multiple projects, I feel overwhelmed and quality suffers. I need us to prioritize together and set realistic timelines. Can we have a weekly planning meeting to align on expectations?
examples.why_it_works: This presents boundaries as quality-enhancing and proposes a collaborative solution.

common_mistakes: Saying yes when you mean no
Overestimating how much time you have
Feeling guilty for protecting your time
Not leaving buffer time between commitments
things_to_avoid: Apologizing for having limits on your availability
Letting others schedule your life for you
Ignoring signs of time-related burnout
Believing that being busy equals being valuable
references: Linehan, M. M. (2015). DBT Skills Training Manual
Alberti, R., & Emmons, M. (2017). Your Perfect Right
Brown, B. (2021). Atlas of the Heart

boundary_types.definition: Limits on technology use, online interactions, and digital privacy
boundary_types.description: Digital boundaries involve managing your relationship with technology and online interactions. This includes screen time limits, social media privacy, online communication expectations, and data sharing. Research indicates that unclear digital boundaries contribute to anxiety, sleep disruption, and relationship conflict (Twenge, 2017).
boundary_types.psychological_background: Digital boundaries are a modern extension of personal boundaries. The constant connectivity enabled by technology creates pressure for 24/7 availability. Neurological research shows that social media triggers dopamine responses similar to addictive substances, making boundary-setting around technology particularly challenging. The APA has issued guidelines on healthy technology use.

examples.user_says: My partner monitors my social media and gets upset about who I follow or what I post.
examples.boundary_response: Digital autonomy is part of personal autonomy. You might say: 'My social media is my space. I'm happy to share what's important with you, but I need you to trust me.'
examples.i_statement: I feel controlled and surveilled when my social media is monitored because it feels like a lack of trust. I would like us to trust each other's digital choices.
examples.dear_man_version: I need to talk about something affecting our trust. When you monitor my social media activity, I feel controlled and like you don't trust me. I'm happy to share things directly with you, but I need my own digital space. Trust is essential for our relationship. Can we agree to respect each other's online autonomy?
examples.why_it_works: This frames digital boundaries as trust-building rather than hiding.

examples.user_says: My coworkers message me on Slack at all hours and expect immediate responses.
examples.boundary_response: Digital work boundaries are essential. You might say: 'I'm available during work hours. For true emergencies, a phone call works. Otherwise, I'll respond in the morning.'
examples.i_statement: I feel anxious and unable to relax when work messages come in after hours because I never truly disconnect. I would like to establish clear communication boundaries.
examples.dear_man_version: I want to discuss our team's communication norms. When messages come in at all hours, I feel unable to disconnect and recharge. I do great work during business hours. For true emergencies, a phone call is best. Otherwise, I'll respond first thing in the morning. This will help me be more refreshed and productive. Can we set this expectation?
examples.why_it_works: This positions boundaries as productivity-enhancing and team-beneficial.

examples.user_says: My 12-year-old wants unlimited phone access. I'm worried about the impact but don't want them to feel left out.
examples.boundary_response: Parental digital boundaries protect developing brains. You might say: 'I care about your wellbeing, and we need reasonable limits. Let's agree on screen-free times together.'
examples.i_statement: I feel concerned when screen time is unlimited because research shows it affects sleep and mood. I would like us to create reasonable limits together.
examples.dear_man_version: I want to talk about phone use in our family. I understand why unlimited access feels important, and I'm concerned about your wellbeing. Research shows too much screen time affects sleep and mood. I need us to set some limits together—like phones off an hour before bed. What do you think would be fair?
examples.why_it_works: This involves the child in boundary-setting while maintaining parental authority.

examples.user_says: My friend posts everything about her life online, including photos I've asked her not to share.
examples.boundary_response: Digital consent is real consent. You might say: 'I need you to ask before posting photos of me. My image is mine to control.'
examples.i_statement: I feel violated when photos of me are shared without permission because I lose control over my image. I would like you to ask before posting anything including me.
examples.dear_man_version: I need to address something important about our friendship. When you post photos of me without asking, I feel like my privacy isn't respected. I need you to ask before sharing anything that includes me. This isn't about being secretive—it's about consent. Can you respect this boundary?
examples.why_it_works: This frames digital consent as a fundamental right rather than a personal quirk.

examples.user_says: I can't stop playing games even when I know I should be doing other things. My family is upset.
examples.boundary_response: Self-boundaries around digital use require compassion and structure. You might say: 'I recognize this is affecting my life. I'm going to set time limits and ask for accountability.'
examples.i_statement: I feel out of control when gaming takes over because it affects my responsibilities. I would like to set limits and get support.
examples.dear_man_version: I want to acknowledge something difficult. Gaming is affecting my responsibilities and relationships. I feel out of control and want to change. I need to set limits on my play time and I'd appreciate your support. Can we establish some check-ins about this?
examples.why_it_works: This combines self-awareness with accountability, making change more sustainable.

common_mistakes: Expecting instant responses to messages
Oversharing personal information online
Not setting parental controls or limits
Confusing online connections with real relationships
things_to_avoid: Checking devices during face-to-face conversations
Comparing your life to social media highlights
Ignoring the impact of screen time on mental health
Letting technology replace real human connection
references: APA Guidelines for Technology and the Family
Brown, B. (2021). Atlas of the Heart

boundary_types.definition: Limits around sexual behavior, consent, and intimate communication
boundary_types.description: Sexual boundaries involve your right to decide what sexual activities you engage in, with whom, and under what circumstances. These boundaries encompass consent, sexual preferences, privacy about sexual matters, and protection from sexual coercion. Clear sexual boundaries are associated with greater sexual satisfaction and relationship health (Kontula, 2009).
boundary_types.psychological_background: Sexual boundaries are fundamental to sexual autonomy and are protected by both ethical guidelines and law. They develop through sexual socialization and are influenced by cultural, religious, and personal values. Trauma can significantly impact sexual boundaries, making therapeutic support often necessary. The APA emphasizes informed consent and client autonomy in all relationships.

examples.user_says: My partner keeps pressuring me to try things I've said no to. I feel guilty and confused.
examples.boundary_response: Consent must be enthusiastic and ongoing. You might say: 'My answer is no, and that needs to be respected. Pressure doesn't change my boundary—it damages our trust.'
examples.i_statement: I feel pressured and anxious when my 'no' isn't respected because it undermines my sense of safety. I would like my boundaries to be respected without pressure.
examples.dear_man_version: I need to talk about something serious. When I say no to sexual activities and you keep asking, I feel pressured and unsafe. My answer is no, and that needs to be the end of it. Pressure damages trust and makes intimacy worse, not better. I need you to respect my boundaries without continued persuasion. This is non-negotiable for our relationship.
examples.why_it_works: This is direct and non-negotiable, as sexual boundaries must be.

examples.user_says: My partner wants to know every detail of my sexual past. I don't think that's necessary.
examples.boundary_response: Sexual privacy is valid. You might say: 'I'm happy to share relevant health information, but my detailed history is private. What matters is our present.'
examples.i_statement: I feel uncomfortable when asked for intimate details because my past is private. I would like to focus on our present relationship.
examples.dear_man_version: I want to address your questions about my past. I'm happy to discuss sexual health and anything that affects our relationship. Detailed history feels private to me and isn't necessary for our connection. What matters is what we build together now. Can we focus on that?
examples.why_it_works: This distinguishes between relevant information and voyeuristic curiosity.

examples.user_says: In the middle of intimacy, I changed my mind but felt I couldn't stop things.
examples.boundary_response: You can always change your mind, even mid-activity. You might say: 'Stop. I need to pause.' Your partner should respect this immediately.
examples.i_statement: I feel scared and trapped when I can't express that I want to stop because my consent can be withdrawn at any time. I would like us to always check in.
examples.dear_man_version: I need to establish a safe word or signal for intimacy. When I want to stop at any point, I need to know you'll respond immediately without questions or guilt. Can we agree that 'stop' or a safe word means everything pauses instantly?
examples.why_it_works: This creates a safety mechanism that protects autonomy during vulnerable moments.

examples.user_says: I'm a survivor and certain touches trigger me. My partner doesn't understand.
examples.boundary_response: Trauma-informed sexual boundaries are essential. You might say: 'Certain touches are difficult for me because of my past. Let's learn together what feels safe.'
examples.i_statement: I feel triggered and scared when certain touches happen because of my trauma history. I would like us to learn what feels safe together.
examples.dear_man_version: I need to share something vulnerable about intimacy. Because of my past trauma, certain touches are triggering for me. This isn't about you—it's about my healing. I want intimacy with you, and I need patience and understanding. Can we learn together what feels safe? A therapist specializing in this could help us.
examples.why_it_works: This normalizes trauma responses and invites collaborative healing.

examples.user_says: My friends always want details about my sex life. I'm not comfortable sharing.
examples.boundary_response: Sexual privacy extends to all relationships. You might say: 'My sex life is private. I'd rather not discuss details.'
examples.i_statement: I feel uncomfortable when asked for details about my sex life because it feels invasive. I would like to keep this private.
examples.dear_man_version: I appreciate that we're close friends, and I need to set a boundary. My sex life is private, and I don't want to share details. I'm happy to talk about relationships in general, but specific details are off-limits. Can you respect that?
examples.why_it_works: This is direct and doesn't require justification.

common_mistakes: Agreeing to things you don't want to please your partner
Thinking you can't change your mind mid-activity
Believing that being in a relationship means automatic consent
Ignoring your body's signals of discomfort
things_to_avoid: Pressuring someone sexually
Ignoring verbal or non-verbal cues of discomfort
Using guilt or manipulation to get sexual compliance
Sharing private sexual details without consent
references: Linehan, M. M. (2015). DBT Skills Training Manual
van der Kolk, B. (2014). The Body Keeps the Score

boundary_types.definition: Limits around possessions, money, and lending
boundary_types.description: Material boundaries involve your right to control your possessions, finances, and lending practices. These boundaries protect you from financial exploitation and establish clear expectations about sharing resources. Research shows that money conflicts are a leading cause of relationship stress, making clear material boundaries essential (Papp et al., 2009).
boundary_types.psychological_background: Material boundaries intersect with self-worth, generosity, and trust. Difficulty setting material boundaries often stems from fear of being seen as selfish or cheap. Cultural and family messages about money significantly influence these boundaries. The framework of 'lending is giving, borrowing is returning' helps clarify expectations.

examples.user_says: My brother asks to borrow money every month and never pays it back. I'm tired of it.
examples.boundary_response: You can love someone without enabling them. You might say: 'I can't lend you money anymore. I'm happy to help you find other solutions.'
examples.i_statement: I feel used and resentful when money is borrowed and not returned because it affects my finances. I would like us to find other ways for me to help.
examples.dear_man_version: I need to talk about our financial relationship. When I lend money and it's not returned, I feel used and resentful. I care about you and want to help, but lending isn't working. I'm happy to help you find other solutions—maybe budgeting help or community resources. Can we explore those options?
examples.why_it_works: This separates the person from the behavior and offers alternative support.

examples.user_says: Friends borrow my things and return them damaged. I'm afraid to say no.
examples.boundary_response: You can lend or not lend based on your comfort. You might say: 'I'm not comfortable lending that item, but I'm happy to let you use it while I'm present.'
examples.i_statement: I feel anxious about lending items because I've had things returned damaged. I would like to either not lend or be present when items are used.
examples.dear_man_version: I want to be upfront about something. When I lend valuable items, I get anxious about their condition. I'm not comfortable lending certain things out anymore. I'm happy to let you use them while I'm present, though. Can we work within that limit?
examples.why_it_works: This offers a compromise that protects your belongings while maintaining the relationship.

examples.user_says: My partner makes large purchases without discussing it with me. I feel disrespected.
examples.boundary_response: Shared finances require shared decisions. You might say: 'Any purchase over $100 needs to be discussed first. Our finances are a team decision.'
examples.i_statement: I feel disrespected and anxious when major purchases are made without discussion because it affects our shared finances. I would like us to agree on a threshold for discussing purchases.
examples.dear_man_version: I need to talk about our financial communication. When large purchases are made without discussion, I feel disrespected and anxious about our finances. I need us to agree on a threshold—maybe $100—where we discuss purchases first. This isn't about control; it's about teamwork. Can we set this standard?
examples.why_it_works: This frames financial boundaries as partnership rather than control.

examples.user_says: My mother-in-law gives gifts but then expects us to do whatever she wants.
examples.boundary_response: Gifts should be freely given. You might say: 'Thank you for the gift. I want you to know that gifts and favors are separate.'
examples.i_statement: I feel manipulated when gifts come with expectations because it doesn't feel like genuine generosity. I would like gifts to be given freely.
examples.dear_man_version: I appreciate your generosity, and I need to clarify something. When gifts come with expectations, it feels less like generosity and more like obligation. I want to enjoy gifts freely without feeling indebted. Can we agree that gifts are given without strings attached?
examples.why_it_works: This distinguishes between genuine generosity and conditional giving.

examples.user_says: Coworkers constantly borrow my supplies without asking. I'm always running out.
examples.boundary_response: Professional material boundaries are reasonable. You might say: 'Please ask before using my supplies. I have limited quantities and need to plan.'
examples.i_statement: I feel frustrated when my supplies are taken without asking because it disrupts my work. I would like you to ask before using my things.
examples.dear_man_version: I wanted to mention something about office supplies. When my supplies are borrowed without asking, I sometimes run short for my own work. Please check with me first—I'm usually happy to share, but I need to plan. Can you ask before taking anything?
examples.why_it_works: This is reasonable and professional while maintaining the boundary.

common_mistakes: Lending money you can't afford to lose
Saying yes out of guilt rather than genuine generosity
Not setting clear repayment expectations
Confusing generosity with self-sacrifice
things_to_avoid: Keeping score of every penny spent
Using money to control others
Lending items you'd be devastated to lose
Ignoring financial boundaries until resentment builds
references: Brown, B. (2021). Atlas of the Heart
Papp, L. M., et al. (2009). Financial Disagreements
Alberti, R., & Emmons, M. (2017). Your Perfect Right

boundary_types.definition: Limits around ideas, opinions, beliefs, and intellectual discourse
boundary_types.description: Intellectual boundaries protect your right to your own thoughts, beliefs, and opinions while respecting others'. These boundaries involve how you engage with disagreements, share ideas, and handle criticism. Clear intellectual boundaries prevent manipulation and maintain intellectual autonomy.
boundary_types.psychological_background: Intellectual boundaries relate to cognitive autonomy and critical thinking. They develop through education, experience, and exposure to diverse perspectives. Threats to intellectual boundaries include gaslighting, intellectual bullying, and forced conformity. The APA emphasizes intellectual freedom and respect for differing viewpoints.

examples.user_says: My partner always tells me my opinions are stupid. I've stopped sharing my thoughts.
examples.boundary_response: Intellectual respect is non-negotiable. You might say: 'My opinions matter, even when we disagree. I need you to respect my right to think differently.'
examples.i_statement: I feel belittled and silenced when my opinions are dismissed because it attacks my intelligence. I would like us to discuss ideas respectfully even when we disagree.
examples.dear_man_version: I need to address how we discuss ideas. When you dismiss my opinions as stupid, I feel belittled and stop sharing. I need intellectual respect in our relationship. We can disagree respectfully without either person being wrong. Can we commit to valuing each other's perspectives?
examples.why_it_works: This establishes that respect is required even in disagreement.

examples.user_says: My boss shuts down any idea that isn't his. I don't feel safe sharing.
examples.boundary_response: Intellectual safety is essential for creativity. You might say: 'I'd like to share an idea, and I need space to present it without immediate judgment.'
examples.i_statement: I feel discouraged from sharing when ideas are dismissed immediately because it stifles creativity. I would like space to present ideas fully before feedback.
examples.dear_man_version: I want to contribute to our team's innovation, and I've noticed it's hard to share ideas. When ideas are dismissed quickly, I feel discouraged. I'd like us to create space where all ideas are presented fully before evaluation. This will help us find better solutions. Can we try this approach?
examples.why_it_works: This frames intellectual boundaries as team-beneficial.

examples.user_says: My family pressures me to adopt their political views. I feel like I can't be myself.
examples.boundary_response: Intellectual autonomy is a right. You might say: 'I respect your right to your beliefs, and I need you to respect mine. We don't have to agree.'
examples.i_statement: I feel pressured and invalidated when my beliefs are challenged because I have a right to think independently. I would like us to respect different viewpoints.
examples.dear_man_version: I love our family, and I need to address something. When I'm pressured to adopt certain beliefs, I feel like I can't be myself. I respect your right to your views, and I need the same respect. We can love each other while thinking differently. Can we agree to disagree sometimes?
examples.why_it_works: This separates love from ideological conformity.

examples.context: Friend who always plays devil's advocate
examples.user_says: My friend argues against everything I say. I feel drained and unheard.
examples.boundary_response: Constant debate is exhausting. You might say: 'I don't need you to challenge everything. Sometimes I just need you to listen.'
examples.i_statement: I feel exhausted when every conversation becomes a debate because I don't always need intellectual challenge. I would like more supportive listening.
examples.dear_man_version: I value our friendship, and I've noticed something. When every conversation becomes a debate, I feel drained and unheard. Sometimes I need you to listen and support rather than challenge. Can we find a balance between discussion and support?
examples.why_it_works: This distinguishes between productive debate and exhausting argumentativeness.

examples.user_says: My partner tells me I'm remembering things wrong. I'm starting to doubt myself.
examples.boundary_response: Gaslighting attacks intellectual boundaries. You might say: 'I trust my memory. You can remember things differently, but that doesn't make me wrong.'
examples.i_statement: I feel confused and anxious when my memories are questioned because it makes me doubt myself. I would like us to trust each other's perceptions.
examples.dear_man_version: I need to address something serious. When you tell me I'm remembering things wrong, I start doubting myself. I trust my memory, and you can remember things differently without me being wrong. I need you to stop correcting my experiences. This is hurting our relationship and my mental health.
examples.why_it_works: This directly addresses manipulation while affirming self-trust.

common_mistakes: Arguing to win rather than to understand
Taking intellectual disagreement personally
Abandoning your beliefs to keep the peace
Confusing being open-minded with having no opinions
things_to_avoid: Intellectually bullying others into agreement
Gaslighting or dismissing others' perceptions
Making someone feel stupid for their beliefs
Using facts to win rather than connect
references: Alberti, R., & Emmons, M. (2017). Your Perfect Right
Brown, B. (2021). Atlas of the Heart

family.situation: Parent who calls multiple times a day expecting immediate response
family.challenge: Family guilt and obligation make this boundary feel disrespectful
family.boundary_script: Mom, I love hearing from you, and I can't always respond immediately. Let's set a regular time to catch up instead.
family.i_statement: I feel overwhelmed when I'm expected to answer every call because I need time to focus. I would like us to schedule regular check-ins.
family.dear_man: I want to maintain our close relationship, and I need to adjust how we communicate. When I'm expected to answer every call immediately, I feel overwhelmed. I'd love a regular Sunday call where we can really talk. This would actually give us better quality time together. Can we try that?

family.situation: Sibling who criticizes your parenting decisions
family.challenge: Family loyalty and shared history make criticism feel like betrayal
family.boundary_script: I appreciate your concern, but my parenting decisions are mine to make. I'll ask for advice when I need it.
family.i_statement: I feel hurt and undermined when my parenting is criticized because I'm doing my best. I would like support rather than criticism.
family.dear_man: I know you care about my kids, and I need to address your comments about my parenting. When I'm criticized, I feel hurt and unsupported. I'm their parent and making thoughtful decisions. I'd love your support rather than criticism. Can we agree on that?

family.situation: Extended family gossip about your personal life
family.challenge: Family gatherings feel unsafe when information spreads without consent
family.boundary_script: I've heard my personal business is being discussed. What I share with family is confidential. Please don't share my information.
family.i_statement: I feel betrayed when personal information is shared without my permission because it damages trust. I would like family information to stay within the family.
family.dear_man: I need to address something important. When I share personal things with family, I expect confidentiality. I've heard my business is being discussed with others. This breaks trust and makes me less likely to share. I need my privacy respected. Can you agree to keep family matters private?

family.situation: Parent comparing you to siblings or cousins
family.challenge: Comparisons trigger childhood wounds and feel invalidating
family.boundary_script: I don't compare well to others. I need you to appreciate me for who I am.
family.i_statement: I feel inadequate when compared to others because it feels like I'm never enough. I would like to be appreciated for my own qualities.
family.dear_man: I want to talk about something that's hurt me for years. When you compare me to others, I feel inadequate and unvalued. I need you to appreciate me for who I am. Comparisons damage our relationship. Can you commit to stopping this?

family.situation: In-law who tries to control holiday plans
family.challenge: Navigating two families' expectations while maintaining your own traditions
family.boundary_script: We've decided to split holidays between our families. This year we'll be with my family for Thanksgiving.
family.i_statement: I feel pressured when holiday plans are dictated because our family deserves a say. I would like us to plan holidays collaboratively.
family.dear_man: I want to discuss holiday planning. I know holidays are important to both families, and we need a fair system. We've decided to alternate years. This year we'll be with my family for Thanksgiving. I value time with your family and need our decisions respected. Can we plan ahead to avoid last-minute conflicts?

family.situation: Family member with addiction who asks for money
family.boundary_script: I love you and can't give you money for substances. I'll drive you to treatment anytime.
family.i_statement: I feel torn between helping and enabling because I love you but don't want to contribute to harm. I would like to support recovery instead.
family.dear_man: I need to be honest because I love you. I can't give you money when I suspect it's for substances. This isn't about not caring—it's about not enabling. I'll drive you to treatment anytime. I want to help your recovery, not fund addiction. Can we find ways to support your health?

romantic_partner.situation: Partner who checks your phone when you're not looking
romantic_partner.challenge: Trust violations feel like betrayal but may stem from insecurity
romantic_partner.boundary_script: My phone is private. If you have concerns, let's talk about them directly.
romantic_partner.i_statement: I feel betrayed when my privacy is invaded because it communicates distrust. I would like us to address concerns openly.
romantic_partner.dear_man: I need to address something serious about trust. When you check my phone, I feel betrayed and mistrusted. If you have concerns about our relationship, I need you to talk to me directly. My phone is private, and invading that privacy damages our trust. Can we agree to discuss concerns openly?

romantic_partner.situation: Partner who makes major decisions without consulting you
romantic_partner.boundary_script: We need to discuss major decisions together. This affects us both.
romantic_partner.i_statement: I feel excluded and disrespected when major decisions are made without me because we're a team. I would like us to decide together.
romantic_partner.dear_man: I need to address how we make decisions. When major choices are made without my input, I feel excluded and like my opinion doesn't matter. We're partners and need to decide together. Can we agree to discuss anything that affects us both?

romantic_partner.situation: Partner who uses silent treatment when upset
romantic_partner.challenge: Emotional withdrawal feels punishing and controlling
romantic_partner.boundary_script: I'm here when you're ready to talk, but silent treatment isn't acceptable. Let's take space and come back to discuss.
romantic_partner.i_statement: I feel punished and anxious when you withdraw without explanation because it leaves me guessing. I would like us to communicate even when upset.
romantic_partner.dear_man: I need to discuss how we handle conflict. When you give me the silent treatment, I feel punished and anxious. I understand needing space, and I need communication about that. Can we agree that if you need space, you tell me 'I need time' rather than disappearing? This will help us both.

romantic_partner.situation: Partner who doesn't respect your 'no' during intimacy
romantic_partner.boundary_script: No means no, always. If you can't respect that, we have a serious problem.
romantic_partner.i_statement: I feel unsafe when my sexual boundaries aren't respected because consent is essential. I would like my 'no' honored immediately.
romantic_partner.dear_man: This is non-negotiable. When I say no sexually and you continue, I feel unsafe and violated. Consent must be enthusiastic and respected. If you can't honor my no, this relationship has a fundamental problem. I need immediate respect for my boundaries.

romantic_partner.situation: Partner who mocks your dreams or goals
romantic_partner.challenge: Emotional support is essential in partnership
romantic_partner.boundary_script: My dreams matter to me. I need you to support them or at least not tear them down.
romantic_partner.i_statement: I feel hurt and discouraged when my goals are mocked because I need encouragement. I would like support even if you don't fully understand.
romantic_partner.dear_man: I need to talk about how you respond to my goals. When my dreams are mocked, I feel hurt and alone. I need encouragement from my partner, or at minimum, respect. If you can't support my goals, I need you not to belittle them. This is important to me. Can you commit to respecting my aspirations?

romantic_partner.situation: Ex-partner who won't stop contacting you
romantic_partner.challenge: Boundaries with exes require firmness and sometimes legal intervention
romantic_partner.boundary_script: I've asked you to stop contacting me. Please respect that. Further contact will be reported.
romantic_partner.i_statement: I feel harassed when contact continues after I've asked for space because I need to move on. I would like my request for no contact respected.
romantic_partner.dear_man: I need to be direct. I've asked you to stop contacting me, and you haven't respected that. This is harassment and I won't tolerate it. Please stop all contact immediately. If it continues, I will involve authorities. This boundary is final.

friends.situation: Friend who only contacts you when they need something
friends.challenge: Uneven friendship feels draining and one-sided
friends.boundary_script: I notice we only talk when you need help. I'd like our friendship to be more balanced.
friends.i_statement: I feel used when I only hear from you during crises because friendships should be reciprocal. I would like balanced connection.
friends.dear_man: I value our friendship and need to address something. When I only hear from you when you need something, I feel used. Friendships need balance. I'd love to hear from you when things are good too, or just to check in. Can we work on making this more mutual?

friends.situation: Friend who gossips about others to you
friends.challenge: Gossip erodes trust and creates三角drama
friends.boundary_script: I'm not comfortable hearing gossip about others. Please don't share that with me.
friends.i_statement: I feel uncomfortable when others are gossiped about because it makes me wonder what you say about me. I would like our conversations to be more positive.
friends.dear_man: I need to mention something about our conversations. When gossip is shared, I feel uncomfortable and wonder what's said about me. I'd prefer we talk about positive things or address issues directly with people. Can you respect that I don't want to hear gossip?

friends.boundary_script: I shared something in confidence. I need to know my secrets are safe with you.
friends.i_statement: I feel betrayed when my secrets are shared because confidentiality is essential to trust. I would like you to keep what I share private.
friends.dear_man: I need to address a trust issue. When I share something in confidence and it gets told to others, I feel betrayed. Confidentiality is essential for friendship. I need to know I can trust you with personal information. Can you commit to keeping what I share private?

friends.situation: Friend who makes every conversation about themselves
friends.boundary_script: I've been listening a lot, and I need to share too. Can we balance our conversations?
friends.i_statement: I feel unheard when conversations are one-sided because I need to be heard too. I would like balanced sharing.
friends.dear_man: I value our conversations, and I've noticed they're often one-sided. When I'm always listening, I feel unheard. I need to share too. Can we work on balancing our conversations so we both feel heard?

friends.challenge: Criticism disguised as concern still hurts
friends.boundary_script: I appreciate your concern, and I need you to support my decisions even if you'd choose differently.
friends.i_statement: I feel criticized and unsupported when my choices are judged because I need acceptance. I would like support even if you disagree.
friends.dear_man: I want to address how you respond to my decisions. When my choices are criticized, I feel unsupported. I need friends who accept me, even when we disagree. I value your opinion, and I need your support more than your judgment. Can you work on that?

friends.situation: Friend who expects you to drop everything for them
friends.boundary_script: I care about you, and I can't always drop everything. Let's plan ahead when possible.
friends.i_statement: I feel stressed when I'm expected to be available constantly because I have my own responsibilities. I would like us to plan ahead when possible.
friends.dear_man: I care about you and our friendship. When I'm expected to drop everything, I feel overwhelmed. I want to be there for you, and I need advance notice when possible. Can we work on giving each other more heads-up for plans?

workplace.situation: Boss who takes credit for your work
workplace.challenge: Professional boundaries in hierarchical settings require tact
workplace.boundary_script: I'd like to present my project directly to leadership to share the full context of the work.
workplace.i_statement: I feel undervalued when my contributions aren't acknowledged because recognition motivates me. I would like credit where credit is due.
workplace.dear_man: I want to discuss how we communicate our team's work to leadership. When my contributions aren't acknowledged, I feel undervalued. I'd like opportunities to present my work directly. This helps leadership understand our capabilities. Can we find ways to highlight individual contributions?

workplace.situation: Colleague who dumps their work on you
workplace.boundary_script: I can help with specific tasks, but I can't take on your core responsibilities.
workplace.i_statement: I feel taken advantage of when I'm expected to do your work because it affects my own responsibilities. I would like clear role boundaries.
workplace.dear_man: I want to clarify our working relationship. When I'm asked to take on your core tasks, it affects my own work. I'm happy to help with specific questions or collaborate on projects, but I can't do your job for you. Can we discuss what's reasonable to ask of each other?

workplace.boundary_script: This feels unsafe. I need to report this to management/safety officer.
workplace.i_statement: I feel concerned about my safety because [specific hazard]. I would like this addressed immediately.
workplace.dear_man: I need to report a safety concern immediately. [Describe hazard]. This creates danger for employees. I need this addressed today. If it's not resolved, I'll need to file a formal report with [safety authority]. Employee safety is non-negotiable.

workplace.challenge: Harassment boundaries require immediate, documented action
workplace.boundary_script: Stop. This is unwelcome. If it continues, I will report it to HR.
workplace.i_statement: I feel harassed and unsafe when [specific behavior] happens because it's unwelcome. I would like it to stop immediately.
workplace.dear_man: I need to be direct. [Specific behavior] is unwelcome and constitutes harassment. I'm telling you to stop. If it happens again, I will report it to HR immediately. This is my final warning. I expect this behavior to end today.

workplace.boundary_script: I'm not well enough to work effectively. I'll be out today to recover.
workplace.i_statement: I feel pressured to work sick because I don't want to let the team down, but I need rest to recover. I would like understanding when I'm ill.
workplace.dear_man: I need to take a sick day today. I'm not well enough to work effectively and need to recover. I'll make sure urgent tasks are covered and return when I'm healthy. Rest helps me return stronger and prevents spreading illness.

workplace.boundary_script: This timeline is unrealistic. Let's discuss priorities and what's achievable.
workplace.i_statement: I feel stressed and set up to fail when deadlines are unrealistic because quality suffers. I would like realistic expectations.
workplace.dear_man: I want to discuss project timelines. When deadlines don't match the work required, quality suffers. I need us to prioritize together and set realistic expectations. What can we delay to focus on what's most important? This will lead to better outcomes.

social_media.boundary_script: I don't engage with harassment. Your comment has been reported.
social_media.i_statement: I feel harassed when [specific behavior] happens because it's unwelcome. I would like it to stop.
social_media.dear_man: I don't engage with harassment or personal attacks. Your behavior violates community standards and has been reported. I'm choosing not to respond further. This conversation is over.

social_media.situation: People sharing your content without permission
social_media.boundary_script: Please remove my content. Sharing without permission violates my boundaries.
social_media.i_statement: I feel violated when my content is shared without permission because I lose control over my words. I would like my content removed.
social_media.dear_man: I need you to remove my content immediately. Sharing my posts/photos without permission violates my boundaries and may violate copyright. I'm asking you to take it down. If it's not removed, I'll report it to the platform.

social_media.situation: Pressure to share personal information online
social_media.boundary_script: I prefer to keep this private. Not everything needs to be shared online.
social_media.i_statement: I feel pressured to overshare because online culture encourages it, but I value my privacy. I would like to share selectively.
social_media.dear_man: I've decided to keep some parts of my life private. Not everything needs to be shared online. I'm choosing what to share on my terms. I hope you can respect that.

social_media.situation: Comparison and FOMO from social media
social_media.boundary_script: I'm taking a break from social media because it's affecting my mental health.
social_media.i_statement: I feel inadequate when I compare myself to curated online personas because it distorts reality. I would like to limit my exposure.
social_media.dear_man: I'm making a decision for my mental health. Social media is affecting how I feel about myself. I'm taking a break to focus on real life. This isn't about anyone else—it's about my wellbeing.

social_media.boundary_script: I've blocked you. Please don't find ways to continue monitoring me.
social_media.i_statement: I feel stalked and unsafe when you monitor my online activity because it's intrusive. I would like you to stop immediately.
social_media.dear_man: I've blocked you for a reason. Monitoring my social media after a breakup is stalking. I need you to stop all monitoring immediately. If this continues, I will involve authorities. This boundary is firm.

self_boundaries.boundary_script: I'm doing my best, and perfection isn't the goal. Progress matters more.
self_boundaries.i_statement: I feel inadequate when I demand perfection from myself because it prevents me from enjoying my achievements. I would like to practice self-compassion.
self_boundaries.dear_man: I need to address how I treat myself. When I'm constantly self-critical, I never feel good enough. I deserve the same compassion I give others. I'm choosing to practice self-acceptance and celebrate progress rather than demand perfection.

self_boundaries.situation: Ignoring your own needs to please others
self_boundaries.boundary_script: I matter too. My needs are as valid as anyone else's.
self_boundaries.i_statement: I feel resentful when I always put others first because I lose myself. I would like to prioritize my needs sometimes.
self_boundaries.dear_man: I need to make a change for my wellbeing. When I constantly put others first, I lose myself. My needs matter too. I'm choosing to balance helping others with caring for myself. This isn't selfish—it's necessary.

self_boundaries.boundary_script: I am enough regardless of my output. Rest is productive too.
self_boundaries.i_statement: I feel anxious when I'm not producing because I tie my worth to productivity. I would like to rest without guilt.
self_boundaries.dear_man: I need to change my relationship with work. When I equate productivity with worth, I never rest. I am enough regardless of my output. I'm choosing to rest without guilt. This will make me more effective in the long run.

self_boundaries.boundary_script: I need to live according to my values, not just please others.
self_boundaries.i_statement: I feel inauthentic when I compromise my values because it affects my self-respect. I would like to live more aligned with my beliefs.
self_boundaries.dear_man: I'm making a commitment to myself. When I compromise my values to please others, I lose myself. I need to live authentically. This might mean some people don't approve, and that's okay. My integrity matters more.

self_boundaries.challenge: Self-care is foundational to everything else
self_boundaries.boundary_script: My health matters. I will prioritize sleep, nutrition, and movement.
self_boundaries.i_statement: I feel depleted when I neglect my health because everything else suffers. I would like to make self-care non-negotiable.
self_boundaries.dear_man: I'm making my health a priority. When I neglect sleep, nutrition, and exercise, everything suffers. I'm committing to non-negotiable self-care. This isn't selfish—it's the foundation for everything else I do.

i_statements.formula: I feel [emotion] when [behavior] because [impact]. I would like [request].
i_statements.description: I-statements express your feelings and needs without blaming others. They reduce defensiveness and promote understanding.

examples.example: I feel disappointed when plans are cancelled last minute because I was looking forward to seeing you. I would like more notice next time.
I feel overwhelmed when I'm handling all the household tasks because it feels unequal. I would like us to divide responsibilities more fairly.
examples.effectiveness_notes: Focuses on impact rather than character

examples.example: I feel undervalued when my contributions aren't acknowledged because recognition motivates me. I would like credit shared appropriately.
I feel overwhelmed when I receive multiple calls daily because I can't focus. I would like us to schedule regular check-ins instead.
I feel uncomfortable when others are gossiped about because it makes me wonder what you say about me. I would like our conversations to stay positive.

variations.when_you_statement: When you [behavior], I feel [emotion] because [impact]. I need [request].
variations.when_you_example: When you raise your voice, I feel anxious because it feels aggressive. I need us to speak calmly.
variations.positive_i_statement: I feel [positive emotion] when [positive behavior] because [positive impact]. I would like more of this.
variations.positive_example: I feel appreciated when you acknowledge my work because it motivates me. I would like more recognition.

dear_man.description: DBT interpersonal effectiveness skill for getting what you want in relationships while maintaining self-respect and the relationship.

steps.description: Describe the current situation objectively, without judgment.
examples: When you arrive an hour late without calling...
When I'm not included in team meetings...
When decisions are made without my input...

steps.description: Express your feelings and opinions about the situation using I-statements.
examples: I feel disrespected when my time isn't valued...
I feel excluded when I'm not part of discussions...
I feel undervalued when my input isn't sought...

steps.description: Assert your wishes clearly and directly. Say what you want.
examples: I need you to call if you'll be more than 15 minutes late...
I want to be included in all team meetings...
I need to be consulted on decisions that affect my work...

steps.description: Reinforce by explaining the positive consequences of getting what you want.
examples: If you call, I won't worry and can plan my time better...
Including me will lead to better collaboration and outcomes...
My involvement will improve decision quality...

steps.description: Stay focused on your goal. Don't get distracted or give in. Use broken record or no technique.
examples: I understand you're busy, and I still need you to call...
I hear your reasons, and my request remains...
I appreciate your perspective, and I need this boundary...

steps.description: Present yourself as confident, even if you don't feel it. Use appropriate eye contact and posture.
examples: Maintain eye contact while speaking
Use a calm, firm tone of voice
Stand or sit with good posture

steps.description: Be willing to give to get. Offer and ask for alternatives.
examples: What if we agree on a 15-minute grace period?
Could we start with weekly meetings and assess?
Would a compromise of [alternative] work for you?

full_example.situation: Partner who never helps with household tasks
full_example.dear_man_script: When I handle all the household tasks alone (Describe), I feel overwhelmed and resentful because it feels unequal (Express). I need us to divide chores more fairly (Assert). If we share responsibilities, I'll have more energy for our time together and feel more appreciative (Reinforce). I know you're busy with work, and this remains important to me (Mindful). [Maintain calm demeanor] (Appear confident). Would you be willing to take on dishes and laundry if I handle cooking and groceries? (Negotiate)

fast.description: DBT skill for maintaining your self-respect during interpersonal interactions.

steps.description: Be fair to yourself AND the other person.
examples: I'm not asking for more than I'm willing to give
This is fair for both of us
I deserve the same respect I give

steps.description: Don't apologize for existing, having needs, or having boundaries.
examples: Don't say 'Sorry to bother you' before a reasonable request
Don't apologize for having feelings
Don't say 'Sorry, but...' when setting a boundary

steps.description: Stay true to your principles and what matters to you.
examples: My integrity matters more than their approval
I won't compromise my values to keep the peace
I deserve to live according to my principles

steps.description: Be honest and accurate. Don't minimize or exaggerate.
examples: I feel [specific emotion], not 'fine' or 'whatever'
This has happened [specific number] of times
I need [specific thing], not 'something'

full_example.situation: Being pressured to do something that violates your values
full_example.fast_script: This request conflicts with my values (Fair). I don't need to apologize for saying no (No unnecessary apologies). My principles are non-negotiable (Stick to values). I cannot do this because [specific reason] (Truthful).

give.description: DBT skill for maintaining positive relationships during conflict.

steps.description: Be gentle—no attacks, threats, or judgments.
examples: I understand you see this differently...
I appreciate your perspective even if I disagree...
Let's find a solution together...

steps.description: Show interest in the other person's perspective.
examples: Help me understand your point of view...
What matters most to you here?
I want to hear your concerns...

steps.description: Acknowledge the other person's feelings and perspective as valid.
examples: It makes sense that you feel that way...
I can see why this is important to you...
Your feelings are understandable...

steps.description: Use humor, warmth, or lightness when appropriate.
examples: Smile appropriately during the conversation
Use a warm, friendly tone
Find common ground to lighten tension

full_example.situation: Disagreeing with your boss about project direction
full_example.give_script: I understand your vision for this project (Gentle). Help me understand your priorities so I can better align my approach (Interested). It makes sense that you're focused on [their concern] given the deadline (Validate). Let's look at this together and find the best path forward (Easy manner).

combined_approaches.description: Combining DEAR MAN, FAST, and GIVE for comprehensive interpersonal effectiveness

example.situation: Asking for a raise while maintaining good relationship with boss

combined_script.DEAR_MAN: I'd like to discuss my compensation (Describe). I've been consistently exceeding my targets and taking on additional responsibilities (Express). I'm requesting a 15% salary increase (Assert). This would reflect my contributions and motivate continued high performance (Reinforce). I understand budget considerations, and this remains important (Mindful). [Present professionally] (Appear confident). Would a phased increase or additional benefits be possible? (Negotiate)
combined_script.FAST: I deserve fair compensation for my work (Fair). I don't need to apologize for asking (No apologies). My contributions have been documented and significant (Stick to values). I've exceeded targets by 20% this year (Truthful).
combined_script.GIVE: I appreciate the opportunities you've given me (Gentle). What are your thoughts on compensation for my role? (Interested). I understand budget constraints are real (Validate). Let's find a solution that works for both of us (Easy manner).

common_boundary_challenges.description: Feeling guilty is normal when changing established patterns. Guilt doesn't mean you're wrong—it means you're growing.
strategies: Remind yourself that boundaries are healthy, not selfish
Remember that guilt fades as boundaries become normal
Seek support from others who respect boundaries

common_boundary_challenges.description: People who benefited from your lack of boundaries will resist changes. This is normal and doesn't mean your boundaries are wrong.
strategies: Stay consistent and don't give in to pressure
Remember that pushback proves the boundary was needed
Have a support system in place
Practice DEAR MAN skills for repeated conversations

common_boundary_challenges.description: Many people avoid boundaries because they fear losing relationships. Healthy relationships survive boundaries; unhealthy ones don't.
strategies: Recognize that boundaries reveal who truly respects you
Build self-worth independent of others' approval
Start with low-stakes boundaries to build confidence
Remember that you deserve relationships that respect your limits

common_boundary_challenges.description: Some cultures or families view boundaries as disrespectful. Your mental health matters more than cultural expectations.
strategies: Start with small boundaries and expand gradually
Frame boundaries in terms of being a better family member
Find allies within the family system
Seek professional support for navigating cultural expectations

references.relevance: Primary source for DEAR MAN, FAST, GIVE skills; evidence-based DBT framework for interpersonal effectiveness

references.title: Your Perfect Right: Assertiveness and Equality in Your Life and Relationships
references.relevance: Foundational text on assertive communication training; practical techniques for boundary setting

references.author: Brown, B.
references.title: Atlas of the Heart
references.relevance: Research on emotional boundaries, vulnerability, and courage in relationships

references.author: Cloud, H., & Townsend, J.
references.title: Boundaries: When to Say Yes, How to Say No to Take Control of Your Life
references.relevance: Comprehensive framework for understanding and setting boundaries in all life areas

references.relevance: Professional ethical standards for therapist boundaries and client autonomy

references.author: van der Kolk, B.

references.relevance: Trauma-informed approaches to boundaries, especially physical and sexual boundaries

references.relevance: Research on digital boundaries and technology's impact on mental health

metadata.evidence_base: Evidence-based framework incorporating DBT, CBT, and humanistic approaches
metadata.intended_use: Counseling AI support for boundary setting education and skill building
